content.text: Fine-tuning takes a pretrained model and continues training it — with all or part of its weights unlocked — on a smaller, labeled target dataset. Unlike feature extraction, which treats the backbone as a frozen function, fine-tuning lets the pretrained representations adapt to the new domain. This extra adaptability typically yields higher accuracy, at the cost of more data, compute, and careful hyperparameter choices.
What Fine-tuning Is
Fine-tuning starts from a pretrained checkpoint — a model whose weights
θ₀
encode general-purpose features learned on a large source dataset — and optimizes those weights further on a target dataset
The crucial difference from training from scratch is the initialization: instead of random weights,
already captures rich structure. The optimizer then makes small, targeted adjustments rather than building representations from nothing.
Fine-tuning exists on a spectrum depending on how many layers are left trainable:
Full fine-tuning:
all backbone layers + new head are trained. Maximum adaptability; requires the most data and compute.
Partial fine-tuning:
early layers remain frozen; only the last N layers and the head are updated. Balances adaptability and data efficiency.
Discriminative fine-tuning:
all layers are trainable but each group gets a different (graduated) learning rate — lower for early layers, higher for later layers. Introduced by ULMFiT, now standard practice.
Why Fine-tuning Works
Pretrained representations sit in a region of parameter space that already captures useful structure. Fine-tuning exploits two properties of this region:
Good initialization.

The pretrained weights are close to a good solution for any task that shares low- to mid-level structure with the source domain. A short gradient descent path connects them to the optimal weights for the target task.
Implicit regularization.

Starting from a meaningful point and using a small learning rate biases optimization toward solutions that do not stray far from the pretrained weights, reducing overfitting on the (often small) target dataset.
Benefits
Benefit
Explanation
Higher peak accuracy
Adapts deep representations to the target domain; consistently outperforms frozen feature extraction when data is sufficient
Bridges domain gaps
Works even when the target domain (e.g. medical images) differs substantially from the source (e.g. ImageNet)
Faster convergence
Reaches good performance in far fewer gradient steps than training from random initialization
Less data than scratch
Needs far fewer labeled examples than a randomly initialized model to match the same accuracy
Trade-offs
Limitation
Explanation
Catastrophic forgetting
High learning rates or long training can overwrite pretrained representations, degrading performance — especially on tasks that depend on general knowledge
Overfitting on small datasets
Unlocking millions of parameters on a dataset of hundreds of examples invites memorization; feature extraction is safer in this regime
More hyperparameters
Learning rate, warmup steps, number of unfrozen layers, and weight decay all interact — tuning takes more effort than simple feature extraction
Higher compute cost
Backpropagating through the full backbone requires storing intermediate activations and running more FLOPs per step than training a small head
Architecture & Gradient Flow

In fine-tuning, gradients from the task loss propagate all the way back through the backbone. The diagram below shows the typical setup — graduated learning rates per layer group and a higher rate on the new head — along with the four main fine-tuning strategies.

attrs.alt: Fine-tuning gradient flow diagram showing discriminative learning rates per layer group and the four main strategies: feature extraction, partial fine-tuning, graduated fine-tuning, and full fine-tuning

content.text: When to Use Fine-tuning
Condition
Recommendation
Target dataset > ~2 000 examples
Fine-tuning likely better than feature extraction
Domain gap between source and target
Fine-tuning necessary to adapt features
Feature extraction already saturated
Unfreeze top layers to unlock more performance
Very small dataset (< ~500 examples)
Use feature extraction; fine-tuning risks overfitting
Large dataset + large domain gap
Full fine-tuning; consider domain-adaptive pretraining first
How to Implement
Vision — PyTorch with discriminative learning rates

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torchvision import models

# 1. Load pretrained backbone
model = models.resnet50(weights=models.ResNet50_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V2)

# 2. Replace the classification head for the new task
num_classes = 10
model.fc = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(model.fc.in_features, 256),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Dropout(0.4),
    nn.Linear(256, num_classes),
)

# 3. Discriminative learning rates: lower for early layers
base_lr = 1e-4
param_groups = [
    {"params": model.layer1.parameters(), "lr": base_lr * 0.1},
    {"params": model.layer2.parameters(), "lr": base_lr * 0.2},
    {"params": model.layer3.parameters(), "lr": base_lr * 0.5},
    {"params": model.layer4.parameters(), "lr": base_lr * 1.0},
    {"params": model.fc.parameters(),     "lr": base_lr * 10.0},  # head trains fastest
]

# layer1's parent (conv1, bn1) — freeze or use very small LR
for p in list(model.conv1.parameters()) + list(model.bn1.parameters()):
    p.requires_grad = False   # freeze stem entirely

optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(param_groups, weight_decay=1e-2)

# 4. Linear warmup + cosine decay scheduler
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import OneCycleLR
scheduler = OneCycleLR(
    optimizer,
    max_lr=[g["lr"] for g in param_groups],
    steps_per_epoch=len(train_loader),
    epochs=20,
    pct_start=0.1,   # 10% warmup
)

# 5. Training loop
model = model.to(device)
loss_fn = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

for epoch in range(20):
    model.train()
    for images, labels in train_loader:
        images, labels = images.to(device), labels.to(device)
        logits = model(images)
        loss = loss_fn(logits, labels)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        scheduler.step()
NLP — HuggingFace Trainer

from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification,
    TrainingArguments, Trainer
)
from datasets import load_dataset
import numpy as np

MODEL = "bert-base-uncased"
NUM_LABELS = 2

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL)
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    MODEL, num_labels=NUM_LABELS
)

# All BERT layers + new classification head are trainable by default
# Optionally freeze the embeddings to save memory
for param in model.bert.embeddings.parameters():
    param.requires_grad = False

dataset = load_dataset("imdb")

def tokenize(batch):
    return tokenizer(batch["text"], truncation=True, max_length=256)

tokenized = dataset.map(tokenize, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"])

training_args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./bert-imdb",
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=16,
    learning_rate=2e-5,          # small LR essential for BERT fine-tuning
    warmup_ratio=0.1,            # 10% linear warmup
    weight_decay=0.01,
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    save_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
)

def compute_metrics(eval_pred):
    logits, labels = eval_pred
    predictions = np.argmax(logits, axis=-1)
    return {"accuracy": (predictions == labels).mean()}

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=training_args,
    train_dataset=tokenized["train"],
    eval_dataset=tokenized["test"],
    compute_metrics=compute_metrics,
)

trainer.train()
Real-World Example

A biotech company needs to classify protein sequences into 50 functional families. They have 40 000 labeled sequences — too few to train a transformer from scratch on protein data, but enough to fine-tune. Their approach:
Load ESM-2 (650M parameter protein language model pretrained on 250 million UniRef sequences). Frozen embeddings, all transformer layers trainable.
Add a classification head: mean-pool over sequence length → Linear(1280, 50).
Fine-tune for 5 epochs with learning rate 1e-4, linear warmup (500 steps), AdamW with weight decay 0.01. Training time: ~2 hours on a single A100.
Result: 94% macro-F1. Feature extraction alone (frozen ESM-2 + linear head) gives 88% macro-F1. Training from scratch on the same 40 000 sequences was not feasible without months of compute.
Common Mistakes
Using too high a learning rate.

A learning rate appropriate for training from scratch (e.g. 1e-3) will destroy pretrained representations in the first few steps. Fine-tuning LRs are typically 10–100× smaller (1e-5 to 3e-4 for transformers).
Skipping warmup.
Jumping to full learning rate from step 0 can cause instability in the first few batches, especially for large transformers. A short linear warmup (1–10% of total steps) stabilizes early training.
Fine-tuning for too many epochs.
Unlike training from scratch, fine-tuning converges quickly. Running for 20+ epochs on a small dataset typically causes overfitting. Monitor validation loss and stop early.
Forgetting to unfreeze all required layers.
Partially loaded checkpoints or framework defaults sometimes freeze BatchNorm or LayerNorm layers unexpectedly, silently degrading performance.
Using the same LR for backbone and head.

The new classification head is randomly initialized and can absorb a larger learning rate, while the backbone needs a small LR to preserve its pretrained knowledge. Using a single uniform LR is a common cause of instability.
Best Practices
Start with feature extraction, then unfreeze.
Train the head-only for 1–2 epochs to warm it up, then unlock deeper layers and continue training with a lower LR. This staged approach avoids large initial gradients corrupting the backbone.
Use discriminative learning rates.
Apply progressively smaller LRs to earlier layer groups (e.g.
for layer group
from the top). Early layers encode general features that rarely need updating; deep layers encode task-specific representations that benefit more from adaptation.
Apply weight decay (L2 regularization).
Weight decay penalizes deviation from the pretrained initialization, acting as a soft anchor. Values around 0.01 (AdamW) work well for most fine-tuning scenarios.
Use early stopping with a patience window.
Validation loss often plateaus or begins rising after 3–10 epochs. Stop when improvement stalls rather than training for a fixed number of epochs.
Data augmentation helps more than in feature extraction.
Because the backbone can adapt its representations, augmented views present genuinely new learning signal — not just noise on top of a fixed embedding.
Mental Model

Think of a pretrained model as an experienced professional hired from a general industry. Feature extraction says: "Your knowledge is perfect as-is — just apply it to our problem." Fine-tuning says: "We'll train you in our specific domain for a few weeks, adjusting your general expertise to our particular workflow." The training period is short and targeted (not a career restart from zero), and the result is someone more effective than either a frozen generalist or a fresh hire trained only in the narrow domain.
Key Takeaways
Fine-tuning continues training a pretrained model on a target dataset, allowing backbone weights to adapt — unlike feature extraction where the backbone is frozen.
It consistently outperforms feature extraction when the target dataset has at least a few thousand examples, especially when there is a domain gap.
The two main failure modes are catastrophic forgetting (LR too high) and overfitting (too many epochs on small data) — both prevented by a small LR, warmup, weight decay, and early stopping.
Discriminative learning rates — lower for early layers, higher for later layers and the new head — are the single most effective structural choice in fine-tuning.
A staged approach — first train the head only, then unfreeze deeper layers — gives the most stable fine-tuning trajectory.
For transformers, standard hyperparameters are: LR ≈ 1e-5 to 3e-4, warmup 5–10% of steps, weight decay 0.01, 2–5 epochs.
